shouldTrigger: Record a blocked marker because the task hit a wall and needs a durable resumable pause.

shouldTrigger: This is a type:change-request that changes a locked architecture decision and needs the approval gate.

shouldTrigger: A required check fails; investigate the root cause before applying any fix.
The runtime behavior surprises us; build a feedback loop before patching.
Before fixing, reproduce the failure, trace the bad value, and confirm the root cause.
shouldNotTrigger: Accept the pull request after reviewing the evidence.
Run grouped closeout and post the status marker after all tasks are complete.
nearMiss: A test is failing; build a reproduction loop before touching the code.

shouldTrigger: Build a landing page for a SaaS product with a clean modern frontend design.

shouldTrigger: Turn repeated agent-process friction from this grouped closeout into skill updates.

shouldTrigger: Use ponytail for this engineer task and take the shortest path that satisfies the task record.
Maintainer, use lazy mode and identify the minimal acceptable scope without dropping acceptance criteria.
Apply YAGNI here: do less, avoid boilerplate, and use the simplest solution that still passes required checks.
The active task record sets minimalism: full, so apply the ponytail minimalism discipline for this engineer task.
Task record minimalism: lite is selected; maintainer should use lazy ponytail discipline without dropping acceptance criteria.
During task creation the source item requests a pluggable framework with one concrete consumer; choose the minimalism level.
shouldNotTrigger: Implement the new behavior by writing a failing test before production code.
Review the implementation artifact against the task record and decide accepted versus needs_revision.
Before claiming done, attach fresh command output from the final state.
nearMiss: Use ponytail and apply YAGNI: implement the new behavior with the smallest useful slice, but start with a failing test and keep RED evidence.

shouldTrigger: Clean up the accepted worktree after integration.

shouldTrigger: Orchestrator, create a task record handoff to the maintainer subagent.

shouldTrigger: Implement the new behavior by writing a failing test before production code.

shouldTrigger: Before claiming the task is done, attach fresh command output from the final state.
Verify the bug is fixed by re-running the original reproduction and reading the output.
Do not accept a passing claim without fresh evidence from the required checks.
The same command on different branch heads is different evidence; a lane-final green run on one head cannot prove the claim for another head.
Baseline evidence may establish starting state for every lane but can never prove a lane-final, integrated, or post-merge claim.
shouldNotTrigger: Draft the architecture decision record.
Generate the Claude Code adapter artifacts.
nearMiss: Before claiming the service works, run the smoke command and read the full output.
Do not cite the baseline run as proof that the integrated composed tree passes its checks.

shouldTrigger: Set up Agentic Loop for the first time in this project and discover which source docs are present.
The source document names are non-standard; use the setup skill to write confirmed overrides to project.md.
Run the one-time setup to detect candidate project docs and record overrides in .agenticloop/project.md.
The project map is unconfirmed; run setup-agenticloop to confirm defaults and write setup_status before the first task.
shouldNotTrigger: Run closeout after all tasks are accepted.
Post the implementation summary under the task record.
nearMiss: Scan for candidate source documents once and ask for confirmation before writing any overrides.

shouldTrigger: We have 4 ready independent tasks, can we parallelize?
Plan a bounded parallel batch and join condition for these ready task records.
Review whether these parallel lanes have disjoint backend objects, leases, and merge barriers.
Two mutation-disjoint tasks share one verified base run; may both parallel lanes cite it to establish baseline state?
Two tasks that edit the same test module are not parallel-write eligible; plan serial writes or one combined lane.
Mutation-disjoint tasks with shared assumptions can still be knowledge-coupled; check knowledge coupling before parallel execution.
Tasks with knowledge coupling use bounded parallel diagnosis, reconciliation at the join, then serial implementation or a re-planned parallel plan.
Route each cross-lane finding to the affected lane and require a disposition before the batch join completes.
This host cannot inject a message into a running parallel lane; route the cross-lane finding at the next checkpoint or join, never claim async delivery.
Both coupled branches are individually green, but the parallel batch still needs combined-state proof through an integration rehearsal.
The integration rehearsal hit a semantic conflict between the two lane artifacts; return a conflict and ordering result for the owning task branches rather than silently resolving it.
The actual merged tree differs from the integration rehearsal candidate; the integrated evidence is stale and must rerun.
Provably disjoint lanes may omit the integration rehearsal when the concurrency plan records the reason.
A passing integration rehearsal never authorizes the actual merge; merge stays a human checkpoint.
shouldNotTrigger: Implement this single task serially and report the result.
Clean up the accepted worktree after integration.
Remove the worktree for the already accepted task.
Run worktree cleanup dry-run for an already accepted task.
nearMiss: Plan parallel delegation for four ready tasks with disjoint backend objects, leases, and a join condition.
The integrated check must bind to the exact combined candidate tree, not to either lane head.
